title: Finding Files
intro: Your project has hundreds of files and you need to find a specific config file buried somewhere in nested folders. Scrolling through the file explorer would take forever. The find command lets you search your entire project in seconds.

teach.title: find = Hiring a Detective to Search Your House
teach.body: Imagine you lost your car keys somewhere in your house. Instead of searching every room yourself, you hire a detective who systematically checks every drawer, shelf, and pocket in every room. You tell them what to look for -- 'silver key with a blue keychain' -- and they report back every match. **The `find` command is that detective** -- you tell it where to search, what to look for, and it checks every folder and subfolder.

teach.title: find = Using a Library Catalog Search
teach.body: At a library, you don't walk through every aisle reading every spine. You use the catalog: search by title, author, or subject, and it tells you exactly which shelf to visit. **The `find` command is your project's catalog** -- you search by name, type, size, or date, and it tells you exactly where matching files live.

teach.title: The find Command
teach.body:
The basic pattern is: **`find WHERE -OPTIONS WHAT`**

- **`find . -name "config.json"`** -- Find files named config.json starting from current directory
- **`find . -name "*.js"`** -- Find all JavaScript files (the `*` matches anything)
- **`find . -type d -name "components"`** -- Find only directories named components
- **`find . -type f -name "*.test.js"`** -- Find only files matching the pattern

The `.` means 'start searching from here.' You can replace it with any path like `/home` or `~/projects`.

teach.title: Finding Things Fast
teach.code:
# Find all JSON config files
find . -name "*.json"

# Find directories named 'test' or 'tests'
find . -type d -name "test*"

# Find all CSS files in the src folder only
find src/ -name "*.css"

# Find files by partial name
find . -name "*config*"
teach.annotation: The -name flag is case-sensitive. Use -iname instead for case-insensitive searches (e.g., finding 'README' whether it's README.md, Readme.md, or readme.md).

teach.body: When a client drops a 500-file codebase on you and says 'fix the API config,' find lets you locate the right file in under a second. That confidence and speed is what separates a vibecoder charging $50/hour from one charging $150/hour.

The find command searches directories recursively for files matching your criteria. Use -name for filenames (with wildcards like *), -type f for files only, and -type d for directories only. Always start with a location (. for current directory).

steps.instruction: Find all JSON files in the current project. We need to know where all our config files are.
steps.hint: Use find with -name and a wildcard pattern: find . -name "*.json"

steps.instruction: Now find all test files. They end with .test.js in this project.
steps.hint: Use the same pattern but with *.test.js to match test files.

steps.instruction: Find all directories named 'components' in the project.
steps.hint: Use -type d to search for directories only, combined with -name.

steps.instruction: Find any file with 'config' anywhere in its name, regardless of extension.
steps.hint: Use wildcards on both sides of the word: find . -name "*config*"

exercise.completionMessage: You can now locate any file in any project instantly! Whether it's a buried config file, scattered test files, or a specific directory, find has you covered. No more scrolling through folders in the file explorer.
